terms.definition: A structured handover framework: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation, used to communicate clinical concerns concisely.
terms.exampleSentence: I am calling using SBAR — situation, Mrs Khan in bed 4 has a NEWS2 of 7.
terms.contextNotes: Endorsed by NHS Improvement and the Royal College of Nursing.

terms.definition: A handover tool extending SBAR with an Identify step (caller and patient identification) at the start.
terms.exampleSentence: Use the ISBAR tool when phoning the on-call team out of hours.
terms.contextNotes: Identify is added because phone callers often skip introductions.

terms.definition: The transfer of professional responsibility and accountability for some or all aspects of patient care to another clinician.
terms.exampleSentence: Please attend the clinical handover at 19:30 in the staff room.
terms.contextNotes: NMC requires accurate, contemporaneous handover.

terms.definition: The act of raising clinical concerns to a more senior clinician when patient deterioration or clinical risk is identified.
terms.exampleSentence: I have escalated to the medical registrar after a sustained NEWS2 of 6.
terms.contextNotes: Document time, person contacted and outcome of escalation.

terms.definition: An emergency call (2222 in NHS hospitals) summoning the resuscitation team for a cardiac or respiratory arrest.
terms.exampleSentence: Put out a crash call — she is unresponsive with no pulse.
terms.contextNotes: NHS standardised emergency number is 2222 across all hospitals.

terms.definition: Medical Emergency Team call: rapid summons of a critical-care-led team to a deteriorating ward patient before arrest.
terms.exampleSentence: Trigger a MET call — his NEWS2 is 9 with new confusion.
terms.contextNotes: Aim is pre-arrest intervention; not all trusts use the term MET.

terms.definition: Do Not Attempt Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation: a clinical decision documenting that CPR will not be attempted in cardiac arrest.
terms.exampleSentence: There is a DNACPR in place dated yesterday and signed by the consultant.
terms.contextNotes: Discuss with patient/family unless harmful; record clearly.

terms.definition: Recommended Summary Plan for Emergency Care and Treatment: a personalised plan including resuscitation and ceiling-of-care decisions.
terms.exampleSentence: Her ReSPECT form specifies ward-level care only with no ITU escalation.
terms.contextNotes: Travels with the patient between care settings.

terms.definition: A legally binding statement under the Mental Capacity Act 2005 in which a competent adult refuses specified future treatment.
terms.exampleSentence: She has an advance decision refusing ventilation, signed and witnessed last year.
terms.contextNotes: Must be in writing, signed and witnessed if refusing life-sustaining treatment.

terms.definition: A legal appointment allowing a nominated person to make health, welfare or financial decisions on behalf of someone who lacks capacity.
terms.exampleSentence: Her son holds a lasting power of attorney for health and welfare.
terms.contextNotes: Must be registered with the Office of the Public Guardian to be valid.

terms.definition: A documented, individualised nursing record of identified needs, planned interventions and expected outcomes.
terms.exampleSentence: Update the care plan to reflect her new pressure-relief mattress.
terms.contextNotes: Reviewed at least every 24 hours per NMC standards.

terms.definition: A bedside or central record summarising a patient's prescribed medications and key nursing instructions.
terms.exampleSentence: Sign the morning round on the kardex once medications are administered.
terms.contextNotes: Term still used widely though many trusts now use ePrescribing.

terms.definition: The legal prescription document on which all administered medicines are signed for each dose.
terms.exampleSentence: Sign the drug chart immediately after administration, never in advance.
terms.contextNotes: Omissions must be coded with the trust omission code.

terms.definition: Medication Administration Record: the document signed at each dose to confirm a medicine has been given to a named patient.
terms.exampleSentence: Initial the MAR chart for each dose given and code any omissions.
terms.contextNotes: Used widely in care homes and community settings.

terms.definition: A 24-hour record of all fluid input (oral, IV, enteral) and output (urine, stoma, drains, vomit) for a patient.
terms.exampleSentence: Maintain a strict fluid balance chart and tally the totals at midnight.
terms.contextNotes: Inaccurate charts are a frequent CQC concern.

terms.definition: A record of stool frequency and Bristol Stool Chart type, used to monitor bowel function and detect infection.
terms.exampleSentence: Document each bowel motion on the stool chart with the Bristol type.
terms.contextNotes: Three or more loose stools in 24h triggers C. diff testing.

terms.definition: A handover conducted at the patient's bedside, allowing the patient to contribute and verifying clinical details.
terms.exampleSentence: We perform bedside handover so patients can hear and correct any inaccuracies.
terms.contextNotes: Ensure patient consent and confidentiality of bay neighbours.

terms.definition: A formal record of a patient safety event, near miss or staff incident submitted via the trust's reporting system.
terms.exampleSentence: Please complete an incident report on Datix for the missed insulin dose.
terms.contextNotes: Datix is the most common NHS reporting platform.

terms.definition: The statutory professional obligation to be open and honest with patients when something goes wrong with their care.
terms.exampleSentence: Apply the duty of candour: explain what happened and what we are doing about it.
terms.contextNotes: CQC Regulation 20 underpins the duty of candour in England.

terms.definition: A serious, largely preventable patient safety incident defined nationally, e.g. wrong-site surgery or misplaced NG tube feed.
terms.exampleSentence: Feeding through a misplaced NG tube is classed as a never event.
terms.contextNotes: List maintained by NHS England; triggers a serious incident review.

terms.definition: A clinical document sent to the GP within 24 hours of discharge, summarising admission, investigations, treatments and follow-up.
terms.exampleSentence: Complete the discharge summary on EDN before the patient leaves the ward.
terms.contextNotes: Required within 24h under NHS standard contract.

terms.definition: To Take Out medications: the supply of discharge medicines dispensed by pharmacy for the patient to take home.
terms.exampleSentence: We are waiting for TTOs from pharmacy before she can be discharged.
terms.contextNotes: Also called TTAs (To Take Away) in some trusts.

terms.definition: A critical care-led nursing team supporting deteriorating ward patients and facilitating ITU admission and step-down.
terms.exampleSentence: I have referred to the outreach team given the rising NEWS2.
terms.contextNotes: Often abbreviated CCOT (Critical Care Outreach Team).

terms.definition: A brief multidisciplinary meeting focused on identifying current risks and patient safety concerns on the ward.
terms.exampleSentence: We hold a safety huddle at 11:00 every morning to flag deteriorating patients.
terms.contextNotes: Promoted by NHS Improvement as a daily safety practice.

terms.definition: A brief MDT meeting, often in front of a status board, planning each patient's day, expected discharge and outstanding actions.
terms.exampleSentence: Please attend the 09:00 board round with your patients' EDD.
terms.contextNotes: EDD = Estimated Date of Discharge.
